0_Overview: What is Rust: Systems Language Focused on Safety and Performance
Benefits: Memory Safety Without a Garbage Collector
Benefits: Zero-cost Abstractions — High-level Code, Low-level Performance
Benefits: Fearless Concurrency — Compiler Prevents Data Races
Benefits: Excellent Error Messages from the Compiler
Benefits: No Null Pointer Exceptions — Option<T> instead
Benefits: No Undefined Behavior in Safe Rust
Benefits: WebAssembly First-class Target
Benefits: Growing Ecosystem (crates.io)
Drawbacks: Steep Learning Curve — Ownership and Borrow Checker
Drawbacks: Slow Compilation Times
Drawbacks: Verbose for Simple Tasks
Drawbacks: Smaller Ecosystem than Go, Python, JavaScript
Drawbacks: Async Story is Complex (multiple runtimes)
When to Use: Systems Programming — OS, Drivers, Embedded
When to Use: WebAssembly Modules
When to Use: High-performance CLI Tools
When to Use: Game Engines and Graphics
When to Use: Networking and Protocol Implementation
When to Use: Replacing C/C++ where safety matters
When NOT to Use: Rapid prototyping, Web backends (Go/Node are simpler), Data Science
Rust vs C++: Safety, Modern Tooling, No UB
Rust vs Go: Performance vs Simplicity
Famous Rust Projects: Firefox (Servo), Deno, SWC, Turbopack, Ripgrep, Tokio
1_Ownership_and_Borrowing: Ownership Rules: One Owner, Drop When Out of Scope
Copy Types: Integers, Booleans, Floats, Char, Tuples of Copy Types
Mutable References: &mut — Only One at a Time
2_Types_and_Pattern_Matching: Scalar Types: i8/16/32/64/128, u8/16/32/64/128, isize, usize, f32/f64, bool, char
Structs: Named, Tuple Structs, Unit Structs
Patterns: Literals, Variables, Wildcards, Ranges, Destructuring
Destructuring Structs, Enums, Tuples in Patterns
3_Traits_and_Generics: Implementing Traits: impl Trait for Type
Trait Bounds: fn f<T: Display + Clone>()
impl Trait in Function Parameters and Return Types
Trait Objects: dyn Trait — Dynamic Dispatch
Box<dyn Trait> for Heap-allocated Trait Objects
Important Standard Traits: Display, Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Hash, From, Into, Iterator
4_Smart_Pointers_and_Memory: Rc<T>: Reference Counting for Multiple Owners
Arc<T>: Atomic Reference Counting for Threads
RefCell<T>: Interior Mutability, Runtime Borrow Checking
RwLock<T>: Multiple Readers or One Writer
Pin<T> and Unpin: Pinning Memory for Async
std::mem: size_of, align_of, swap, replace, take
5_Error_Handling: thiserror Crate: Derive Macros for Error Types
anyhow Crate: Flexible Error Handling for Applications
Error Handling Best Practices: Libraries vs Applications
6_Iterators_and_Closures: Iterator Adapters: map, filter, flat_map, take, skip, zip, enumerate, chain, peekable
Consuming Adapters: collect, sum, product, count, any, all, find, position, max, min, fold, reduce
collect() into Vec, HashMap, String, etc.
7_Async_Rust: Tokio Channels: mpsc, oneshot, broadcast, watch
Axum: Web Framework Built on Tokio

FFI: Calling C from Rust with extern
